title: Frontend Developer
description: A guided path from HTML, CSS, and JavaScript fundamentals to React, build tooling, accessibility, testing, and performance.

sections.title: Web & HTML Foundations
sections.description: Understand how the web actually works and learn to structure content with meaningful, valid HTML.

topics.title: How the Web Works
topics.description: The core plumbing of the web: browsers, servers, HTTP requests and responses, DNS, and what happens when you type a URL.
topics.why: Every frontend bug, performance issue, and security concern eventually traces back to this mental model. Knowing how requests travel from browser to server lets you debug with confidence instead of guessing, and it is the foundation for topics like caching, CORS, and deployment.
learn: Trace what happens when you enter a URL: DNS lookup, TCP/TLS connection, HTTP request, response, render
Learn the anatomy of an HTTP request and response: methods, status codes, and headers
Understand what DNS does and how domain names map to servers
Open the Network tab in your browser and inspect real requests on a site you use daily
criteria: You can explain the request/response cycle from typing a URL to pixels on screen
You can identify common HTTP status codes (200, 301, 404, 500) and what they mean
You inspected a real page load in the Network tab and read its headers

topics.title: HTML Basics
topics.description: The markup language of the web: elements, attributes, document structure, links, images, and lists.
topics.why: HTML is the skeleton every website hangs on — CSS and JavaScript both operate on it. Writing clean, valid HTML from day one prevents an entire class of styling, scripting, and accessibility problems later.
learn: Write a valid HTML document by hand: doctype, head, body, headings, and paragraphs
Use links, images, lists, and tables with correct attributes
Learn how the browser parses HTML into a document tree
Validate your markup and fix every error it reports
criteria: You built a multi-page site with only HTML, hand-written in a text editor
You can explain the difference between block-level and inline elements
Your pages pass an HTML validator with no errors

topics.title: Semantic HTML
topics.description: Choosing elements for their meaning — header, nav, main, article, section, footer — rather than styling convenience.
topics.why: Semantic markup is what screen readers, search engines, and browser reader modes rely on to understand your page. It is the cheapest accessibility and SEO win available, and it makes your HTML dramatically easier to style and maintain.
learn: Learn the landmark elements (header, nav, main, aside, footer) and when to use each
Use the correct heading hierarchy (h1-h6) to outline a document
Replace div-soup in one of your pages with semantic elements
Understand when a div or span is actually the right choice
criteria: You can justify the element choice for every tag in one of your pages
You refactored a div-only layout into landmarks and a logical heading outline
You can explain how semantic markup helps screen reader users navigate

topics.title: Forms & Validation
topics.description: Collecting user input with form elements, labels, input types, and built-in browser validation.
topics.why: Forms are how the web does business — logins, checkouts, searches, signups. Native input types and constraint validation give you huge functionality for free, and forms done wrong are the single most common accessibility failure in real products.
learn: Build a form using labels correctly associated with inputs
Use specialized input types (email, number, date, url) and see how mobile keyboards adapt
Apply built-in validation with required, pattern, and min/max, and style :invalid states
Understand how form submission works with GET vs POST
criteria: You built a signup form with labels, fieldsets, and native validation
You can explain when a form should use GET versus POST
Your form is fully operable with only the keyboard

sections.title: CSS & Layout
sections.description: Style and lay out pages with modern CSS: the cascade, Flexbox, Grid, responsive design, and scalable architecture.

topics.title: CSS Fundamentals
topics.description: Selectors, the cascade, specificity, inheritance, the box model, colors, and units.
topics.why: The cascade and the box model explain 90% of 'why does my CSS not work' moments. Once specificity and box sizing click, CSS stops feeling random and starts feeling like a system you control.
learn: Master selectors: type, class, attribute, pseudo-classes, and combinators
Learn how the cascade resolves conflicts: origin, specificity, and source order
Internalize the box model: content, padding, border, margin, and box-sizing
Practice with relative units (rem, em, %, vw/vh) and know when to avoid px
criteria: You can predict which rule wins in a specificity conflict without testing
You can diagram the box model of any element using DevTools
You styled a full page from scratch without a framework

topics.title: Flexbox & Grid
topics.description: The two modern layout systems: Flexbox for one-dimensional flow and Grid for two-dimensional page structure.
topics.why: Flexbox and Grid replaced a decade of float hacks with layout tools that actually match how designers think. Nearly every real-world component — navbars, cards, sidebars, galleries — is a few lines of Flexbox or Grid once you know them.
learn: Build a navbar and a card row with Flexbox: direction, justify, align, gap, and wrap
Build a page shell with Grid: template columns/rows, areas, and gap
Learn when to reach for Flexbox versus Grid
Complete Flexbox Froggy and Grid Garden to drill the properties

resources.label: CSS-Tricks — A Complete Guide to Flexbox
CSS-Tricks — A Complete Guide to Grid

criteria: You built a holy-grail page layout (header, sidebar, content, footer) with Grid
You can center anything, horizontally and vertically, without googling
You can articulate when Flexbox is the better tool than Grid and vice versa

topics.title: Responsive Design
topics.description: Making layouts adapt to any screen with fluid sizing, media queries, and mobile-first thinking.
topics.why: More than half of web traffic is mobile, and your users will open your site on everything from a watch to an ultrawide monitor. Responsive design is not a feature — it is the baseline expectation for professional frontend work.
learn: Adopt a mobile-first workflow: style small screens, then enhance with min-width media queries
Use fluid techniques (max-width, minmax, clamp, flexible images) so layouts bend before they break
Set the viewport meta tag and understand why it matters
Test a page across breakpoints with DevTools device emulation
criteria: You built a page that works from 320px to 1920px without horizontal scrolling
You can explain mobile-first versus desktop-first and defend your choice
You used clamp() or minmax() to reduce the number of media queries needed

topics.title: Scalable CSS Architecture
topics.description: Keeping styles maintainable as projects grow: naming conventions like BEM, CSS custom properties, and preprocessors.
topics.why: CSS is easy to write and brutal to maintain — unscoped styles rot fast in team codebases. Conventions, design tokens via custom properties, and tooling like Sass keep a growing stylesheet predictable instead of terrifying to touch.
learn: Adopt a naming convention such as BEM and apply it to a component set
Define design tokens (colors, spacing, type scale) with CSS custom properties
Try a preprocessor like Sass for nesting and partials, and know what native CSS now covers
Learn how utility-first frameworks like Tailwind approach the same problem
criteria: You built a small component library styled with a consistent naming convention
You themed a page (light/dark) by swapping custom property values only
You can explain the trade-offs between BEM, utility-first, and CSS-in-JS approaches

sections.title: JavaScript & the DOM
sections.description: Learn the language of the browser and use it to read, modify, and react to the page.

topics.title: JavaScript Fundamentals
topics.description: Core language features: variables, types, functions, objects, arrays, control flow, and scope.
topics.why: JavaScript is the only language browsers execute natively, and every framework you will ever use is just JavaScript underneath. Deep fundamentals are what separate developers who debug frameworks from developers who are debugged by them.
learn: Learn variables (let/const), primitive types, and type coercion pitfalls
Write and compose functions; understand parameters, return values, and arrow syntax
Work fluently with objects and arrays, including map, filter, and reduce
Understand scope, closures, and the this keyword
Solve small problems daily until the syntax disappears
criteria: You can explain closures with an example you wrote yourself
You can transform data with map/filter/reduce without writing loops
You solved 20+ small JavaScript exercises without reference material

topics.title: DOM Manipulation & Events
topics.description: The Document Object Model: selecting, creating, and updating elements, and responding to user events.
topics.why: The DOM is the live, scriptable representation of your page — it is what makes the web interactive. Understanding it directly, before frameworks abstract it away, demystifies what React and friends are actually doing for you.
learn: Select elements with querySelector/querySelectorAll and read or change their content and attributes
Create, insert, and remove elements; update classes and styles from JavaScript
Handle events with addEventListener and read the event object
Learn event bubbling and implement event delegation on a list
Build a small interactive widget (tabs, modal, or todo list) with no libraries
criteria: You built a working todo app in vanilla JavaScript with add, complete, and delete
You can explain event bubbling and when to use event delegation
You can update the page from user input without a single page reload

topics.title: Async JavaScript & Fetch
topics.description: Handling operations that take time: the event loop, promises, async/await, and fetching data from APIs.
topics.why: Real apps live on data from servers, and JavaScript handles that without freezing the page through its async model. Promises and async/await are non-negotiable for any frontend job, and misunderstanding the event loop is behind some of the gnarliest bugs you will meet.
learn: Understand the event loop: call stack, task queue, and why JavaScript never blocks on I/O
Work with promises: then/catch, chaining, and Promise.all
Rewrite promise chains with async/await and handle errors with try/catch
Fetch JSON from a public API and render it into the DOM, including loading and error states
criteria: You built a page that fetches API data and shows loading, success, and error states
You can explain what the event loop does using a concrete example
You can convert between promise chains and async/await fluently

topics.title: Modern JavaScript & Modules
topics.description: ES6+ features you will see everywhere — destructuring, spread, template literals, classes — and organizing code with ES modules.
topics.why: All modern codebases and every React tutorial assume fluency in ES6+ syntax. Modules are how real projects split code across files, and they are the concept bundlers, imports, and code-splitting are all built on.
learn: Practice destructuring, spread/rest, template literals, and optional chaining until they are second nature
Split a project into ES modules with import and export
Understand default versus named exports and module scope
Learn what transpilation is and why tools like Babel and modern bundlers exist
criteria: You refactored a single-file script into clean ES modules
You can read modern framework code without tripping on syntax
You can explain the difference between default and named exports

topics.title: Browser DevTools
topics.description: Your in-browser debugger and inspector: the Elements, Console, Sources, Network, and Performance panels.
topics.why: DevTools is the single highest-leverage skill multiplier in frontend work — it turns every 'it doesn't work' into an inspectable, steppable, measurable situation. Developers who master it fix in minutes what others chase for hours.
learn: Inspect and live-edit HTML and CSS in the Elements panel, including computed styles
Set breakpoints and step through code in the Sources panel instead of console.log-ing everything
Analyze requests, payloads, and timing in the Network panel
Record a Performance profile and read the flame chart at a basic level
criteria: You debugged a real bug using breakpoints rather than console.log
You can find why an element has a given style using the Computed panel
You diagnosed a slow or failing request from the Network panel

sections.title: Tooling & Workflow
sections.description: Adopt the professional toolchain: version control, package management, bundlers, and automated code quality.

topics.title: Git & GitHub
topics.description: Version control for tracking changes, branching safely, and collaborating through commits and pull requests.
topics.why: Every professional team runs on Git — it is as fundamental as the text editor. It gives you fearless experimentation (everything is undoable) and is the backbone of code review, CI, and deployment workflows.
learn: Learn the core loop: init, status, add, commit, log, and diff
Create branches, merge them, and resolve a merge conflict on purpose
Push to GitHub, open a pull request, and review a diff
Practice recovering: amend, revert, and restore
criteria: You use feature branches and merge via pull requests on your own projects
You resolved a merge conflict without panic or data loss
You can read git log/diff output to reconstruct what changed and when

topics.title: npm & Package Management
topics.description: Node.js-based tooling: installing dependencies, package.json, semantic versioning, lockfiles, and npm scripts.
topics.why: The entire frontend ecosystem — React, Vite, ESLint, everything — ships as npm packages. Understanding package.json, versioning, and lockfiles is the difference between a reproducible project and 'works on my machine'.
learn: Install Node.js and initialize a project with package.json
Add, update, and remove dependencies; understand dependencies vs devDependencies
Read semantic versioning (^, ~, exact) and explain what the lockfile guarantees
Write npm scripts to run your common tasks
criteria: You set up a project from scratch with package.json and npm scripts
You can explain what ^1.2.3 permits and why lockfiles matter
You diagnosed and fixed a dependency issue using npm ls or a clean install

topics.title: Build Tools & Bundlers
topics.description: Tools like Vite and webpack that bundle modules, run dev servers with hot reload, and produce optimized production builds.
topics.why: Modern apps are hundreds of modules that must become a few optimized files for the browser. Knowing what your bundler does — transforming, bundling, minifying, code-splitting — lets you fix broken builds and shrink slow ones instead of treating tooling as magic.
learn: Scaffold a project with Vite and explore the dev server, hot module replacement, and build output
Understand what bundling, transpiling, and minification each do and why they exist
Configure environment variables and static assets in a build
Compare the dev build with the production build and inspect what changed
criteria: You built and served a production bundle and can describe what the tool did
You can explain why raw ES modules alone are not enough for large production apps
You fixed a build error by reading the tool's output rather than re-scaffolding

topics.title: Linting & Formatting
topics.description: Automated code quality with ESLint and consistent style with Prettier, wired into your editor and scripts.
topics.why: Linters catch real bugs — unused variables, unsafe equality, broken hook rules — before code ever runs. Formatters end style debates permanently, and teams expect both to be running on every save and every commit.
learn: Add ESLint to a project, run it, and fix every warning it raises
Add Prettier and configure your editor to format on save
Understand the division of labor: linters for correctness, formatters for style
Wire lint and format checks into npm scripts
criteria: Your projects lint clean and format automatically on save
You can explain two real bug classes ESLint catches
You added a lint script that fails on warnings, suitable for CI

sections.title: React
sections.description: Build component-based user interfaces with React: components, state, hooks, routing, data, and TypeScript.

topics.title: React Fundamentals
topics.description: Components, JSX, props, and rendering: describing UI as a function of data.
topics.why: React is the most widely used frontend framework, and its mental model — declarative UI built from composable components — transfers to nearly every modern framework. It is also the single most requested skill in frontend job listings.
learn: Scaffold a React app with Vite and understand every file it generates
Write function components with JSX and compose them into a UI tree
Pass data with props and render lists with keys
Render conditionally and follow the 'Thinking in React' process to break a design into components
criteria: You built a multi-component React app that renders dynamic lists from data
You can explain what JSX compiles to and why keys matter in lists
You can decompose a mockup into a sensible component hierarchy

topics.title: State & Hooks
topics.description: Making components interactive with useState, synchronizing with external systems via useEffect, and the rules of hooks.
topics.why: State is what turns static markup into an application, and hooks are how all modern React code manages it. Understanding rendering — when and why components re-run — is what keeps your apps correct and fast instead of glitchy and slow.
learn: Manage component state with useState and update it immutably
Handle user events and build controlled form inputs
Use useEffect for data fetching and subscriptions, with correct dependency arrays and cleanup
Lift state up to share it between components
Learn the rules of hooks and why they exist
criteria: You built an interactive app (e.g., searchable list or quiz) with useState and controlled inputs
You can explain what triggers a re-render and trace one through your app
You fetch data in useEffect with proper loading, error, and cleanup handling

topics.title: Routing & Data Fetching
topics.description: Building multi-page single-page apps with client-side routing and managing server data with a fetching library.
topics.why: Real apps have URLs, navigation, and remote data with caching, revalidation, and error handling. A router plus a server-state library like TanStack Query is the standard architecture of production React apps, and it eliminates whole categories of hand-rolled fetching bugs.
learn: Add client-side routing with routes, links, nested layouts, and URL params
Handle not-found routes and redirects
Adopt a data-fetching library and learn queries, caching, and invalidation
Distinguish server state from client state and store each appropriately
criteria: You built a multi-route app with a detail page driven by URL params
You can explain what a query cache does and when data refetches
Navigation in your app preserves state and works with the browser back button

topics.title: TypeScript for React
topics.description: Static types for JavaScript: annotating variables and functions, typing props and state, and catching errors at compile time.
topics.why: TypeScript is the default in professional frontend codebases — most React job postings expect it. Types turn runtime surprises into instant editor feedback and make refactoring large component trees safe instead of scary.
learn: Learn basic annotations: primitives, arrays, objects, unions, and type inference
Define interfaces or type aliases for component props and useState generics
Type event handlers and API response data
Convert one of your JavaScript React projects to TypeScript
criteria: You migrated a React app to TypeScript with zero any types in your own code
You can read a compiler error and fix the underlying type mismatch
You typed an API response and caught a shape mismatch before runtime

sections.title: Accessibility & Testing
sections.description: Build interfaces everyone can use and prove they work with automated tests.

topics.title: Accessibility Fundamentals
topics.description: Making sites usable by people with disabilities: perceivable, operable, understandable, robust — the principles behind WCAG.
topics.why: Over a billion people live with disabilities, accessibility is a legal requirement in many markets, and accessible sites are simply better-built sites. Most of it costs nothing when done early: correct elements, labels, contrast, and keyboard support.
learn: Learn the POUR principles and skim the WCAG success criteria structure
Navigate your own site using only the keyboard and fix every trap or missing focus style
Check color contrast and text alternatives for images
Run an automated audit (Lighthouse or axe) and fix what it finds
criteria: Your site is fully operable via keyboard with visible focus indicators
You fixed all issues from an automated accessibility audit on one project
You can explain the four POUR principles with concrete examples

topics.title: ARIA & Assistive Technology
topics.description: Using ARIA roles, states, and properties correctly to make custom widgets understandable to screen readers.
topics.why: When you build custom components — menus, dialogs, tabs — semantics no longer come for free, and ARIA is how you restore them. Used well it makes rich UIs accessible; used carelessly it makes them worse, so the rules matter.
learn: Learn the first rule of ARIA: prefer native HTML elements before reaching for roles
Study accessible widget patterns (dialog, tabs, menu) in the ARIA Authoring Practices Guide
Add appropriate aria-label, aria-expanded, and live regions to a custom component
Test one of your pages with an actual screen reader (VoiceOver, NVDA, or Orca)
criteria: You built an accessible custom widget (e.g., modal or tabs) following the APG pattern
You used a screen reader to verify your component announces state changes
You can explain why 'no ARIA is better than bad ARIA'

topics.title: Unit & Component Testing
topics.description: Writing automated tests for functions and React components with Vitest/Jest and Testing Library.
topics.why: Tests are what let you refactor and ship on a Friday without fear — they catch regressions the moment they appear. Testing Library's user-centric approach also pushes you toward more accessible components as a side effect.
learn: Write unit tests for pure functions: arrange, act, assert
Test React components with Testing Library: query by role, simulate user events, assert output
Mock network requests so tests are fast and deterministic
Run tests in watch mode during development and add a test script for CI
criteria: You wrote a component test suite covering rendering, interaction, and an async fetch
You can explain why querying by role beats querying by test id
A test in your suite caught a real regression you introduced

topics.title: End-to-End Testing
topics.description: Testing whole user flows in a real browser with tools like Playwright or Cypress.
topics.why: Unit tests can all pass while the login flow is broken — E2E tests verify the app actually works the way users experience it. A handful of well-chosen E2E tests over critical paths is the highest-confidence safety net a frontend team has.
learn: Set up Playwright or Cypress and write a first test that loads your app
Script a critical user journey: navigate, fill a form, submit, and assert the result
Learn locator strategies and why auto-waiting beats manual sleeps
Run E2E tests headlessly and debug failures from traces or screenshots
criteria: You automated a full user flow that passes headlessly from the command line
You debugged a failing E2E test using its trace, video, or screenshot output
You can articulate the testing pyramid and where E2E tests fit

sections.title: Performance & Shipping
sections.description: Make it fast, keep it safe, and get it in front of users: performance, security, and deployment.

topics.title: Web Performance & Core Web Vitals
topics.description: Measuring and understanding page speed: loading, interactivity, and visual stability via LCP, INP, and CLS.
topics.why: Slow sites lose users and revenue measurably — performance is a product feature, not a nice-to-have. Core Web Vitals also feed Google's ranking, so speed directly affects how many people ever find your site.
learn: Learn the three Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) and what user experience each protects
Run Lighthouse on one of your projects and read the full report
Understand the critical rendering path: how HTML, CSS, and JS block or unblock first paint
Distinguish lab data from field data and why both matter
criteria: You can name the three Core Web Vitals and their good-score thresholds
You measured a page with Lighthouse and identified its top bottleneck
You can explain render-blocking resources and one way to mitigate them

topics.title: Asset & Code Optimization
topics.description: Shrinking what you ship: responsive images, modern formats, lazy loading, code splitting, and caching.
topics.why: Images and JavaScript are the two heaviest things on the web, and optimizing them yields the biggest performance wins available. These are the concrete techniques that turn a red Lighthouse score green.
learn: Serve responsive images with srcset/sizes and modern formats like WebP or AVIF
Lazy-load below-the-fold images and set width/height to prevent layout shift
Split code by route with dynamic import and measure the bundle size difference
Understand HTTP caching headers and what a CDN adds

resources.label: Cloudflare Learning — What is a CDN?

criteria: You cut a page's transfer size significantly and documented before/after numbers
You implemented route-based code splitting in a React app
You can read response headers and explain how an asset is cached

topics.title: Web Security Essentials
topics.description: Frontend-relevant security: HTTPS, XSS, CSRF, CORS, Content Security Policy, and safe handling of user input.
topics.why: The frontend is the attack surface users touch — one XSS hole can leak every user's session. Employers expect frontend developers to know the OWASP basics, and understanding CORS alone will save you days of confused debugging.
learn: Understand what HTTPS/TLS protects and why every site needs it
Learn how XSS works and how escaping, frameworks, and CSP defend against it
Learn CSRF and the role of SameSite cookies and tokens
Demystify CORS: same-origin policy, preflights, and why the browser blocks that request
Skim the OWASP Top 10 and map each risk to a frontend implication
criteria: You can explain XSS and CSRF with an example attack and its defense
You diagnosed and correctly fixed a CORS error without disabling security
You completed at least one hands-on lab on XSS or CSRF

topics.title: Deployment & CI
topics.description: Getting your app onto the internet: static hosting, environment configuration, and automated build-and-deploy pipelines.
topics.why: A project on localhost helps no one — shipping is the skill that turns practice into a portfolio. Modern platforms make production deploys with CI, previews, and HTTPS nearly free, and knowing the pipeline end-to-end makes you the person who can unblock releases.
learn: Deploy a static site or React build to GitHub Pages, Netlify, or Vercel
Connect deploys to Git so every push builds and ships automatically
Configure environment variables per environment and understand preview deployments
Add your lint and test scripts as CI checks that gate the deploy
criteria: You have a project live on a public URL with HTTPS, deployed automatically from Git
Your pipeline runs tests and lint before deploying, and a failure blocks the release
You can walk someone through your app's full path from commit to production
